title: Method Overloading 📚
description: Methods that share the same name.

slides.title: The Naming Problem 📝
slides.content:
Imagine you have a method `add(int a, int b)`.

But what if sometimes you want to add three numbers together? Or what if you want to add two decimals (`double`s)?

Do you have to name them all differently? `addTwoInts()`, `addThreeInts()`, `addTwoDoubles()`? That sounds awful.

slides.title: Overloading to the Rescue 🦸‍♂️
slides.content:
## Method Overloading

In Java, multiple methods **CAN share the exact same name**... AS LONG AS they have different parameter types or quantities.

Java is smart enough to look at the arguments you pass in, and select the correct version of the method.

slides.title: Seeing it Work 👀
slides.content:
Notice how all three methods are named `add`. 

Java looks at what I put in the parentheses and runs the matching block.

quizData.question: Can I have two methods `static int multiply(int a, int b)` and `static double multiply(int x, int y)` in the same class?

options.text: Yes, because the return types are different.
No, because the parameter lists (two ints) are identical.
Yes, because the variable names inside the parentheses are different.

quizData.explanation: Overloading strictly relies on the PARAMETERS being different (different types or different amounts). Changing the return type or parameter names is not enough to differentiate them for Java.
